SemiAnalysis.text:
**New this period:**
- A SemiAnalysis contractor found hundreds of plausible bugs in LLVM and NVIDIA's ptxas compiler using AI agents and fuzzing techniques.
- The contractor used ChatGPT 5.5 and Claude to write and optimize a fuzzer, which found bugs at a significantly higher rate than expected.
- The cost of using AI agents to find bugs was over $10,000 in a few hours, but the potential value of finding critical bugs was deemed to be higher.

**Details:**
**Finding Miscompiles for Fun, Not Profit (https://newsletter.semianalysis.com/p/finding-miscompiles-for-fun-not-profit)**
- The author, a contractor at SemiAnalysis, worked on compilers for ML at Google, Waymo, and OpenAI, and has experience with CUDA support in clang, XLA:GPU, Triton, and OpenAI's custom hardware.
- The contractor used a fuzzer to find bugs in LLVM and ptxas, and later used AI agents (Claude) to read through the LLVM code and find bugs at a rate of one every four minutes.
- Named companies and their roles: 
  - Google: previous employer of the contractor
  - Waymo: previous employer of the contractor
  - OpenAI: previous employer of the contractor and provider of custom hardware
  - SemiAnalysis: current employer of the contractor
  - NVIDIA: provider of ptxas compiler
  - AMD: provider of AMDGPU backend for LLVM
- Quantitative figures:
  - $10,000: cost of using AI agents to find bugs
  - $200/month: cost of ChatGPT Pro account
  - $1000: cost of using Opus 4.7 for a few days' work
  - 40: number of programs that ptxas miscompiles found by the fuzzer in three days
  - 80: number of programs that ptxas miscompiles found by the fuzzer after a week
  - 20%: proportion of x86 bugs examined by the author
  - 30: number of agent-discovered bugs examined by the author
  - 50: number of subagents used by Claude to find bugs
- Supply chain and value chain relationships:
  - SemiAnalysis uses tokens from ChatGPT Pro and Opus 4.7 to power its AI agents
  - The contractor uses Claude, a large language model, to write and optimize the fuzzer
- Technology transitions:
  - The use of AI agents to find bugs in compilers is a new and emerging trend
  - The contractor notes that the cost of using AI agents is high, but the potential value of finding critical bugs is also high
- Competitive positioning:
  - The contractor notes that companies that can afford to use AI agents to find bugs will have an advantage over those that cannot
- Capital allocation signals:
  - SemiAnalysis is investing in tokens for AI agents to power its bug-finding efforts
- Regulatory or policy signals:
  - None mentioned in the article

Andrej Karpathy \u2013 Blog.text:
**New this period:**
- Andrej Karpathy releases a new art project called microgpt, a single file of 200 lines of pure Python that trains and infers a GPT model.
- The microgpt project simplifies large language models to their bare essentials and is available on GitHub and as a Google Colab notebook.
- The project uses a dataset of 32,000 names and a simple tokenizer that assigns one integer to each unique character in the dataset.

**Details:**
**[microgpt](http://karpathy.github.io/2026/02/12/microgpt/)**
- The core thesis of this piece is the release of microgpt, a simplified large language model implemented in a single Python file.
- Companies mentioned: GitHub, Google (for Google Colab notebook).
- The specific role of GitHub is to host the microgpt source code, and the specific role of Google is to provide the platform for the Google Colab notebook.
- Quantitative figures: 200 lines of code, 32,000 names in the dataset, 26 possible lowercase characters (a-z) in the tokenizer, and 1 special token (BOS) for a total vocabulary size of 27.
- Supply chain or value chain relationships: the project relies on the GitHub platform for hosting the code and the Google Colab notebook for interactive execution.
- Technology transitions: microgpt simplifies large language models by reducing them to their essential components, making it easier to understand and implement these models.
- The project uses a simple tokenizer and autograd engine, which are key components of large language models, and demonstrates their usage in a simplified setting.
- The autograd engine is implemented using a Value class that wraps scalar numbers and tracks how they were computed, allowing for automatic differentiation and gradient computation.
- No specific capital allocation signals or regulatory signals are mentioned in this piece.

Ethan Mollick \u2013 One Useful Thing.text:
**New this period:**
- AI-generated writing is increasingly prevalent, but can lack human meaning and undermine the development of important human tasks like writing.
- Using AI as a default or without thinking can lead to "cognitive surrender" and hinder learning.
- AI can be a useful tool for learning when used properly, such as with guided learning modes.
- The way AI tools are designed can encourage or discourage cognitive surrender.

**Details:**
**Choosing to Stay Human (https://www.oneusefulthing.org/p/choosing-to-stay-human)**
- The author argues that while AI can be a useful tool for writing and learning, over-reliance on it can lead to a lack of human meaning and undermine the development of important human tasks.
- Companies mentioned: 
  - ChatGPT (provider of AI writing tools)
  - Anthropic (provider of AI tools, including Claude)
  - Google (implied, as the developer of Gemini)
  - Boston Consulting Group (user of AI tools)
- Quantitative figures:
  - 0.15 standard deviations (the improvement in student scores when using AI tutoring)
  - 6-9 months (the equivalent of additional schooling achieved through AI tutoring)
  - 758 (the number of consultants participating in an experiment on AI use)
- Supply chain or value chain relationships:
  - ChatGPT provides AI writing tools to users
  - Anthropic provides AI tools, including Claude, to users
  - Boston Consulting Group uses AI tools in its consulting work
- Technology transitions:
  - AI writing tools are replacing human writing in some areas, such as social media posts and academic papers
  - AI tutoring is being used to supplement human teaching
- Competitive positioning:
  - Companies that design AI tools to encourage human thought and effort may have a competitive advantage over those that do not
- Capital allocation signals:
  - Investment in AI research and development is likely to continue, with a focus on creating tools that support human learning and effort
- Regulatory or policy signals:
  - None explicitly mentioned, but the author notes that the defaults being set for AI use will be hard to reverse once a generation of workers and students has built habits around them.

Simon Willison.text:
**New this period:**
- SQLite has added an AGENTS.md file to clarify its policy on accepting code from agents, stating it does not accept agentic code but welcomes bug reports and documentation patches.
- Anthropic and OpenAI are rumored to have found product-market fit with their coding agent products, leading to significant revenue growth from enterprise customers.
- Anthropic and OpenAI have changed their pricing models to charge enterprise customers API prices, resulting in higher costs for companies using their services.
- Uber and Microsoft have reportedly experienced budget overruns and cancellations related to their use of AI services, potentially due to the new pricing models.

**Details:**
**SQLite AGENTS.md and AI-Related Developments**(https://simonwillison.net/2026/May/27/sqlite-agents/#atom-everything and https://simonwillison.net/2026/May/27/product-market-fit/#atom-everything)
- SQLite has added an AGENTS.md file to its codebase, stating it does not accept pull requests without prior agreement and does not accept agentic code.
- Anthropic and OpenAI are rumored to have found product-market fit with their coding agent products, such as Claude Code and Codex.
- Anthropic has changed its pricing model to $20/seat/month plus API pricing for usage, with existing customers being notified of the change upon contract renewal.
- OpenAI has also changed its pricing model, updating its Codex rate card to align with API token usage instead of per-message pricing.
- The new pricing models have resulted in higher costs for companies using these services, with some companies experiencing budget overruns.
- Anthropic and OpenAI are planning to IPO, with Anthropic rumored to hit $10.9 billion in revenue in the second quarter and potentially operating at a profit for the first time.
- Companies such as Uber and Microsoft have reportedly experienced issues with their AI budgets, with Uber "maxing out its full year AI budget" due to Claude Code usage.
- Anthropic has entered into a Cloud Services Agreement with SpaceX, agreeing to pay $1.25 billion per month for compute capacity through May 2029.
- API revenue is becoming less important for Anthropic, with the company shifting its focus to enterprise sales and support.
- Key players involved include:
  - SQLite: developer of the SQLite database
  - Anthropic: developer of Claude Code and other AI products
  - OpenAI: developer of Codex and other AI products
  - Uber: user of Claude Code and other AI services
  - Microsoft: user of Claude Code and other AI services, also developing its own Copilot CLI agent
  - SpaceX: provider of compute capacity to Anthropic
  - Cursor: user of Anthropic's API and developer of its own models
  - GitHub: user of Anthropic's API and developer of Copilot
- Quantitative figures:
  - $1.25 billion: Anthropic's monthly payment to SpaceX for compute capacity
  - $10.9 billion: Anthropic's rumored revenue in the second quarter
  - $1,199.79: estimated cost of using Anthropic's Claude Code for 30 days
  - $980.37: estimated cost of using OpenAI's Codex for 30 days
  - $200+: monthly cost of using Anthropic's or OpenAI's enterprise plans
  - 703: number of open jobs at OpenAI
  - 229: number of open jobs at OpenAI related to enterprise sales and support
  - 390: number of open jobs at Anthropic
  - 105: number of open jobs at Anthropic related to enterprise sales and support
- Technology transitions:
  - Shift from per-message pricing to API token usage for OpenAI's Codex
  - Introduction of new coding agent products, such as Claude Code and Codex
  - Increased use of AI services by companies, leading to budget overruns and cancellations
- Supply chain and value chain relationships:
  - Anthropic and OpenAI provide AI products and services to companies like Uber and Microsoft
  - SpaceX provides compute capacity to Anthropic
  - Cursor and GitHub use Anthropic's API and develop their own models and products

**New this period:**
- Brendan Greeley discusses the 500-year history of the US dollar on the Odd Lots podcast.
- The origins of the dollar are tied to a Spanish coin called the dollar, which was derived from the German word "thaler".
- The concept of monetary sovereignty is complex, and the US did not have complete control over its currency at its founding.

**Details:**
**Brendan Greeley on the Real 500-Year History of the Dollar (https://omny.fm/shows/odd-lots/brendan-greeley-on-the-real-500-year-history-of-the-dollar)**

- The dollar has its roots in a Spanish coin that was widely used globally, including in the American colonies, and was called the dollar due to its German origins.
- The US did not have a domestic source of gold or silver at its founding and instead pegged its currency to the existing global coin.
- Alexander Hamilton referred to this coin as the "ancient dollar", which was already 250 years old at the time.
- The story of the dollar is not just about the US or its financial institutions, but about the currency itself and its evolution over time.
- Greeley's research took him to archival work and reading secondary sources, and he eventually decided to pursue a PhD in history to better understand the subject.

Arxiv \u2013 Trading & Market Microstruc.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers introduce KTD-Fin, a benchmark for evaluating large language model (LLM) trading agents that addresses issues with memorization and attribution.
- A new framework called AlphaForgeBench is proposed for evaluating LLMs in trading strategy design, focusing on generating executable alpha factors rather than discrete trading actions.
- Studies derive the unique linear Kyle equilibrium under noise-perturbed order-flow observation, identifying a "privacy subsidy" that represents a transfer from the protocol's liquidity pool to traders.
- Research extends the privacy-subsidy concept to different models, including the Glosten-Milgrom sequential-trading model and continuous-time Kyle model, demonstrating robustness across various microstructure models.

**Details:**
**From Knowing to Doing: A Memory-Controlled Benchmark for LLM Trading Agents on Stock Markets** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28359)
- Introduces KTD-Fin, a benchmark that uses a data-side masking protocol to anonymize key identifiers and calendar information, separating historical market memory from investment decision-making.
- Evaluates ten frontier LLM agents on the Chinese CSI300 over a 2024-2026 window, finding that masking substantially changes agent rationales, pushing them towards anonymized factor-based reasoning.
- Incorporates a Barra-style performance attribution framework to decompose portfolio returns into market, style, and stock-selection alpha components.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study focuses on the Chinese CSI300 index.

**AlphaForgeBench: Benchmarking End-to-End Trading Strategy Design with Large Language Models** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.18481)
- Proposes AlphaForgeBench, a framework that requires LLMs to generate executable alpha factors and compose factor-based trading strategies grounded in financial knowledge.
- Decouples reasoning from execution mechanics, enabling deterministic and reproducible evaluation while remaining aligned with real-world quantitative research workflows.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study aims to improve the evaluation of LLMs in trading strategy design.

**The Privacy Subsidy: Kyle's $\lambda$ under Noise-Perturbed Order-Flow Observation** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15746)
- Derives the unique linear Kyle equilibrium when a committed Bayesian market maker observes order flow perturbed by independent Gaussian privacy noise.
- Identifies a "privacy subsidy" that represents a transfer from the protocol's liquidity pool to traders, which is the break-even fee any privacy-aggregated exchange must charge.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study focuses on shielded AMMs with explicit additive-noise injection.

**The Privacy Subsidy in Glosten-Milgrom: Bid-Ask Spread and Welfare under Flip-Noise Direction Observation** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19742)
- Derives a closed-form bid-ask spread and welfare decomposition for the Glosten-Milgrom sequential-trading model under a binary flip channel of probability $\eta$.
- Identifies a per-trade transfer $\mu\eta\Delta$ from the protocol's liquidity pool to traders, which is the "privacy subsidy".
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study focuses on MPC-based matching engines with $\varepsilon$-differentially-private direction disclosure.

**The Privacy Subsidy in Continuous-Time Kyle: Cumulative Welfare under Noise-Perturbed Order-Flow Observation** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25631)
- Extends the closed-form privacy-subsidy result to continuous-time, with a committed Bayesian automated market maker observing the aggregate order flow perturbed by an independent Brownian privacy channel.
- Derives the cumulative expected transfer from the protocol's liquidity pool to traders over $[0,1]$ as $|\Pi_M| = \sigma_v \sigma_\varepsilon^2 / \sqrt{\sigma_u^2 + \sigma_\varepsilon^2}$.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study focuses on committed-AMM exchanges under privacy-aggregated information environments.

Arxiv \u2013 Statistical Finance (q-fin..text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers propose geometric observables for detecting financial regime shifts, outperforming classical and machine-learning baselines.
- A Bayesian approach is developed for out-of-sample network reconstruction, enabling prediction of future network configurations with uncertainty quantification.

**Details:**
**Geometric Observables for Financial Regime Detection**(https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.17117)
- The study evaluates four geometric observables (Berry Phase Rate, Spectral Entropy, Reduced State Purity, and Hamiltonian Sensitivity) for detecting financial regime shifts.
- The Berry Phase Rate achieves a median Cohen's d = 0.72, with a 95% percentile-bootstrap CI of [0.34, 1.18], and produces approximately 67% fewer false alarms per year than a label-supervised Random Forest.
- Reduced State Purity attains the highest in-sample separability (d = 0.83), followed closely by the Absorption Ratio (d = 0.80).
- The study uses equity-index returns from 17 historical crises spanning 2000-2024.
- No specific companies are mentioned.

**A Bayesian approach to out-of-sample network reconstruction**(https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.21869)
- The study develops a Bayesian approach for out-of-sample network reconstruction, using past network snapshots to inform a prior and predict subsequent ones.
- The method is instantiated with a single-parameter fitness model and applied to the Electronic Market for Interbank Deposit across the years 1999-2012.
- The method accurately recovers the number of connections per bank at subsequent times, outperforming probabilistic benchmarks.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study focuses on the interbank deposit market.
- The study uses a dataset from the years 1999-2012, with a single-parameter fitness model.

Arxiv \u2013 Portfolio Management (q-fin.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers propose a regime-aware portfolio allocation framework using Hidden Markov Models and Reinforcement Learning to dynamically allocate assets.
- A unified framework for option pricing and term-structure simulation is developed, integrating stochastic volatility, jumps, and rates models.
- Deep learning methods are applied to forecast the U.S. aggregate bond index, with results showing that persistence and stationarity of the series determine predictive performance.
- A correlation-aware benchmark for LLM-driven portfolio management is introduced, evaluating models on their ability to generate diversified portfolios.
- A portfolio optimization benchmark framework is proposed for evaluating large language models in financial decision-making contexts.
- Investor risk profiles of large language models are investigated, revealing differences in risk tolerance and responsiveness to personas.

**Details:**
**Regime-Based Portfolio Allocation Using Hidden Markov Models and Reinforcement Learning** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27848)
- The study proposes a regime-aware portfolio allocation framework using Markov switching models and Reinforcement Learning.
- The framework allocates assets across equities (SPY), long-term Treasuries (TLT), and gold (GLD) using daily ETF data from 2004-2025.
- A three-state Gaussian Hidden Markov Model is estimated, with regimes exhibiting strong persistence and state-dependent return dynamics.
- The study finds that SPY dominates in stable regimes, while TLT and GLD provide protection during stressed periods.
- The Reinforcement Learning policy achieves the highest risk-adjusted performance, with a 30% out-of-sample test window and a one-day execution lag.

**Stochastic Volatility, Jumps, and Rates: A Unified Framework for Option Pricing and Term-Structure Simulation** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27945)
- The study develops an integrated stochastic modeling framework for pricing short and medium-maturity equity options and assessing interest-rate risk.
- The framework uses the Heston, Bates, and CIR models, with calibration using the Lewis Fourier inversion and the Carr-Madan FFT approach.
- The study finds that jump intensities converge to values effectively equal to zero for 60-day maturities, and that continuous stochastic volatility dominates near-term pricing dynamics.
- The CIR short-rate model is calibrated to the Euribor term structure, generating positive and economically consistent forward-rate scenarios.

**Deep Learning Forecasting of the U.S. Aggregate Bond Index** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27977)
- The study applies deep learning methods to forecast the U.S. aggregate bond index using daily observations from 2018 to 2026.
- The study finds that index levels are extremely persistent and consistent with unit-root behavior, while log returns are covariance-stationary with weak linear dependence and pronounced volatility clustering.
- The study evaluates two neural paradigms: Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) trained on Gramian Angular Field (GAF) image encodings.
- The study finds that MLPs match the strong naive persistence benchmark on levels, collapse toward near-zero forecasts on returns, and achieve the strongest incremental performance on the fractionally differenced series.

**PortBench: A Correlation-Aware, Full-Pipeline Benchmark for LLM-Driven Portfolio Management** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27887)
- The study introduces PortBench, a benchmark for LLM-driven portfolio management, spanning six heterogeneous asset classes over ten years.
- PortBench consists of a static QA dataset of 6,269 correlation-based questions and a dynamic five-stage allocation pipeline.
- The study evaluates ten frontier LLMs, finding that despite strong performance on static financial QA, 90% of model-profile combinations fail to outperform a basic equal-weight allocation.

**Constructing a Portfolio Optimization Benchmark Framework for Evaluating Large Language Models** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09301)
- The study introduces a benchmark framework for evaluating the financial decision-making capabilities of large language models (LLMs) through portfolio optimization problems.
- The framework generates a large set of multiple-choice questions by varying objectives, candidate assets, and investment constraints.
- The study compares the performance of GPT-4, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and Llama 3.1-70B, revealing distinct performance patterns and highlighting the potential and limitations of LLMs in finance.

**Investor risk profiles of large language models** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09303)
- The study investigates how large language models (LLMs) form and express investor risk profiles, examining three LLMs (GPT, Gemini, and Llama).
- The study finds that LLMs are generally long-term investors but exhibit different tendencies in risk tolerance, with Gemini having a moderate risk level and Llama skewing more conservative.
- The study finds that assigning specific personas leads each LLM to adjust its risk profile, although the extent of these adjustments differs across the models.
